sources.notes: Statement and constraints: choose up to two drains in a one-dimensional terrain cross-section; sum n <= 2000.
Used to confirm the single-drain contribution formula and the two-drain overlap as cnt[argmax on the interval].
Comments around the official editorial were used only as a cross-check for the overlap formula.

reference_candidates.notes: Candidate Luogu solution page; not used as evidence in this review.

annotation.summary: Choose up to two drain positions to maximize the number of removed water cells, counting water drained by both positions only once.
annotation.constraints: Sum n <= 2000 and h can be very large, so the intended solution works with column heights and interval maxima rather than grid cells.
annotation.core_idea: For one drain at i, the drained amount contributed by column j is h - max(a[min(i,j)..max(i,j)]). Precompute this cnt[i] for every single drain by expanding left and right with a running maximum. For two drains i <= j, their overlap is exactly the one-drain region of an index where a reaches a maximum on [i,j], so the union value is cnt[i] + cnt[j] - cnt[argmax]. Enumerating all pairs is sufficient.
tricks: reduce connected water to interval maxima
overlap equals the basin of the maximum ridge between drains
pair brute force after one-drain preprocessing

solution_variants.name: quadratic-overlap-bruteforce
solution_variants.summary: Precompute cnt[i] for every drain by maintaining running interval maxima, then enumerate all drain pairs and subtract cnt at an argmax between them.

tags.evidence: After deriving the pair-union formula, the accepted solution directly enumerates all O(n^2) drain pairs under the sum n <= 2000 bound.
The decisive observation is that the overlap of two drained regions is cnt[argmax a[i..j]], converting a geometric union into one subtraction.
Single-drain values are computed by maintaining running interval maxima and accumulating each column's water contribution.
